summary: Commercial craft includes claim discipline: every strong promise must be either sourced, visually demonstrated, or softened.
principles: Separate objective claims (measurable, verifiable) from emotional impressions (subjective, experiential) and treat them differently.
Block superlatives ('best,' 'fastest,' 'number one'), quantified results ('2x faster'), and competitor comparisons unless the brief supplies substantiation.
Convert unsupported certainty into experiential language ('feels faster') or remove it entirely.
Every visual that implies a product capability must be achievable in real use — generated visuals that exaggerate performance are compliance violations.
Testimonial and social-proof claims require disclosure of typical results; unrepresentative outcomes are deceptive.
Fine print is not a cure for misleading main content — if the main claim is deceptive, a disclaimer doesn't fix it.
Generated visuals carry special compliance risk because they can show capabilities the product doesn't have — hallucination checks on every product-visible scene.
apply_when: The ad contains performance, health, beauty, productivity, safety, finance, or comparative claims.
The user provided selling points but not substantiation.
Generated visuals could imply more than the product can prove.
avoid_when: The script uses only subjective mood and brand identity language.
The campaign already has approved legal copy for each claim.
The brief explicitly marks a claim as fictional or parody.
failure_patterns: Superlative that can't be substantiated: 'the best camera on any phone' when no independent test supports the claim.
Generated visual that implies a capability: AI-generated footage showing a product feature that doesn't exist in the real product.
Before/after that exaggerates results: the visual contrast implies a transformation the product can't deliver for most users.
Testimonial without disclosure: showing exceptional results without noting 'results may vary' or 'sponsored content.'

cross_domain_notes.note: The hook's visual contrast must not exaggerate beyond what the product can actually deliver — a compliance violation in the first second is fatal.
Proof must be proportional to evidence: if the evidence is weak, the claim must be softened, not the proof made more dramatic.
Product hero continuity must not drift into product misrepresentation — consistency is about accuracy, not idealization.

execution_techniques: Create a claim inventory at the script stage: list every claim, tag it as objective or subjective, and verify each objective claim has evidence.
Run hallucination checks on every product-visible generated clip: compare against the reference image and flag any capability the clip implies.
Use experiential language framing for unsupported claims: 'Designed to feel faster' instead of '2x faster.'
Include a compliance checkpoint in the production bible that lists every claim and its substantiation status before any asset generation.
